# B08: Stakeholder Intelligence

## Section 1: Foundational Profile

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Name** | Alex Forman |
| **Title** | Founder |
| **Organization** | Parse |
| **Product/Service** | Parse turns any webpage into a structured API instantly — enabling developers and non-developers alike to extract data from websites without writing scraping infrastructure. |
| **Motivation** | Democratizing software creation by eliminating the API gap in underserved industries. Deeply moved by the realization that people from non-technical sectors have had product ideas for decades but lacked the developer ecosystem to build them. |
| **Funding Status** | Unknown — needs enrichment. Team has at least one engineer on O-1 visa. Bay Area HQ. |
| **Key Challenges** | Wildly variable backend costs (bandwidth + anti-bot measures vary per request, per site, per query), making pricing extremely difficult. Same API call can cost 100x more depending on result set size (1 product vs 10,000). Currently transitioning from monthly credit buckets to usage-based pricing. |
| **Standout Quote** | "I talk to people who work in the garbage collection who had an idea for an app for 20 years in the garbage collection industry, but I've never been able to build it. And then they also realize, oh, there's no developer ecosystem for garbage, so there's no APIs for interfacing with the garbage collection apps." |

## Section 2: What Resonated

### Non-Technical ICP as a Discovered Market (Not Hypothesized)
- **Quote:** "That has been a huge thing and like, I mean, totally makes sense now."
- **Why it resonated:** Alex admitted he "honestly didn't even think about" the non-technical user segment when starting Parse — it emerged organically from real usage. This wasn't a pitch hypothesis; it's a validated discovery.
- **Signal/Implication:** Parse's non-technical ICP is experientially validated, not theoretical. This makes the Zo partnership narrative much stronger — it's demand-pull, not supply-push.

### The "Unsexy Industry" Democratization Thesis
- **Quote:** "We actually see it a ton because people are just building these industries that there's been no technical progress in, but there's also no APIs."
- **Why it resonated:** Alex lit up describing how people from untouched industries are suddenly building software. He's seeing firsthand what V has been advocating — that inclusiveness in tech isn't just ethical, it's a massive untapped market.
- **Signal/Implication:** Deep mission alignment. Alex isn't just building a scraping tool — he genuinely cares about enabling previously locked-out builders.

### Zo as a Logic Layer for Parse
- **Quote:** "Yeah, there's more logic as well... you don't really need to think about, oh, like, what's our rule set for this? Because you have LLMs that can actually just do this reasoning."
- **Why it resonated:** When V described using Zo to add intelligence on top of Parse APIs (watch lists, auto-dropping stale items), Alex immediately grasped the LLM-as-logic-layer concept and got visibly excited.
- **Signal/Implication:** Alex sees Zo not just as a distribution channel but as a genuine capability multiplier for Parse users. This is partnership-grade alignment, not just a marketing collab.

### Usage-Based Pricing Convergence
- **Quote:** "Yeah, we actually, we've been like credit monthly base but the plan is to swap to usage-based likely like this week."
- **Why it resonated:** V's hypothesis that Zo and Parse pricing models would "love each other" was validated in real-time.
- **Signal/Implication:** Business model alignment. Both platforms benefit from increased usage — a natural flywheel for partnership.

### V's Fitbit Health Demo
- **Quote:** "Yeah, this is really cool."
- **Why it resonated:** When V showed how Zo aggregates health data from Fitbit with genetic profiles, Alex saw the concrete evidence of "non-technical person orchestrating complex data flows." This was the moment Parse + Zo clicked for him.
- **Signal/Implication:** Live demos of real personal use cases are Alex's conversion trigger — abstract pitches won't work as well.
